content.full_text:
State ~f California 
Memorandum 
To 
Members of the Legal, Enforcement 
and Technical Assistance Divisions 
Date 
October 28, 1976 
From 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Tea Pr im 
Sub~~ The requirement in Section 84305 that certain mass 
mailings be sent to the Commission. 
The second sentence in Section 84305 provides " ... 
a copy of every mass mailing in support of or in opposition 
to a state candidate or state measure shall be sent to the 
Commission." 
A mailing will not be subject to this provision 
unless it meets two requirements. 
First, a mailing will not be "in support of or in 
opposition to" a state candidate or measure unless the cost 
of the mailing is an expenditure. 
Thus, for example, a 
regularly published newsletter need not be sent to the Com-
mission since costs related to the newsletter are not expen-
ditures pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section l8225(c)(4)(C). 
Second, even if the costs of the mailing are ex-
penditures, the mailing need not be sent to the Commission 
unless it is a communication which expressly advocates a 
result in an election for or against a clearly identified 
state candidate or state measure, or taken as a whole, it 
unambiguously urges such a result. 
See part 2 of the opinion 
requested by Ralph Juvinall, 2 FPPC Ops. 110 (Aug. 3, 1976, 
No. 75-018). 
Thus, not every mailing made by a candidate, 
controlled committee, or primary purpose committee need be 
sent to the Commission. 
If the two above criteria are met, a copy of the 
mailing must be sent to the Commission regardless of whether 
the sender files campaign statements. 
Therefore, it is 
conceivable that a copy of a mass mailing must be sent to 
the Commission even though the posting requirements set 
rth in the first sentence of Section 84305 need not be 
observed by the sender. 
However, when S.B. 1362 becomes 
effective in January, 1977, the first sentence of Section 
84305 will apply to any mass mailing for 
ich an ex 
nditure 
has been made r 
ardless of whether that expe 
iture is 
"reportable," i.e. regar61ess of 
ether t 
sender files 
campaign statements. 
TP:olh 


/ 
To 
Telephone Memo 
Date 
1/2/76 
From 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Natalie v7est 
Subject: 
SENATOR ROBERTI CALLED ~'1ITH SEVERAL QUESTIONS 
(1) 
Dates for filing campaign statements for the 
4th Assembly District special election: 
The Senator made a contribution to a candidate who lost 
in the primary -- when is the final statement due? 
He has to file statements in Los Angeles County (his 
district), Sacramento County (\vhere the candidate resides), 
and with the Secretary of State. 
Section 81005. 
When is 
the last statement due? 
2 Cal.Adm.Code Section 18421 
provides that the final statement is due 65 days after 
the general election when the candidate runs in the 
primary but does not participate in the general election. 
Apparently there is continuing confusion about 18421. 
He stated that he had received incorrect advice from 
the L.A. County registrar-recorder and the L.A. Secretary 
of State. 
(2) 
What is the period covered by the statement of economic 
interests? 
January 7, 1975 thru January 8, 1976 (inclusive) 
and due no later than February 8. 
This answer is via 
Nancy Salzman who says that he took office on the first 
Monday in January, 1973, but the first Monday was New 
Year so the official date of assuming office and the 
anniversary date is January 8. 
(3) 
Can a lobbyist attend a fundraiser when his employer 
gives him a ticket and tells him to attend? 
Yes, as long as the lobbyist played no part in purchasing 
the ticket or arranging for his employer to purchase the 
ticket. 
NE\'l:pvp

sections.question_synthetic: The document addresses multiple questions: (1) filing deadlines for campaign statements in a special election, (2) the period covered by statements of economic interests, and (3) whether a lobbyist may attend a fundraiser when their employer provides a ticket.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC provides guidance on campaign statement filing deadlines (65 days after general election per Section 18421), clarifies the economic interest statement period (January 7, 1975 through January 8, 1976, due by February 8), and confirms that a lobbyist may attend a fundraiser if they had no role in purchasing or arranging the ticket.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains two distinct memoranda: (1) internal FPPC guidance memo dated October 28, 1976 on Section 84305 mass mailing requirements with no Q/C format, and (2) telephone memo dated 1/2/76 documenting Senator Roberti's questions and FPPC responses. No formal question/conclusion sections found; content extracted from memo body text.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The document addresses multiple questions: (1) filing deadlines for campaign statements in a special election, (2) the period covered by statements of economic interests, and (3) whether a lobbyist may attend a fundraiser when their employer provides a ticket.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC provides guidance on campaign statement filing deadlines (65 days after general election per Section 18421), clarifies the economic interest statement period (January 7, 1975 through January 8, 1976, due by February 8), and confirms that a lobbyist may attend a fundraiser if they had no role in purchasing or arranging the ticket.

embedding.summary: This document contains two memoranda addressing inquiries from Senator Roberti regarding campaign filing deadlines, economic interest statements, and lobbyist attendance at fundraisers, as well as internal FPPC guidance on mass mailing requirements under Section 84305.
